strategy_type: Graph Strategy: We currently have no collected data about what Elon Musk is saying about Twitter. The highest-priority gaps are (1) identifying his recent, direct statements referencing Twitter, (2) placing those statements in chronological context (including replies and threads), and (3) understanding broader topical/trend context to interpret the statements. To close these gaps efficiently and meet the mandate to use diverse endpoints, the next round should combine a targeted content search (tweets that explicitly mention Twitter), a full chronological view of his recent activity (to catch indirect references or follow-ups), and a trend snapshot (to see if Twitter or related topics are part of wider conversation). These three complementary endpoints will quickly yield the raw statements, surrounding conversation, and context needed for a focused deep dive next.

Graph Strategy: Current investigation lacks any confirmed Elon Musk posts that explicitly mention 'Twitter' and also lacks timeline context and profile metadata needed to pull tweet IDs and conversation threads. To close those gaps efficiently and avoid repeating failed searches, the next round should (1) run a narrowly targeted latest-only search for tweets that explicitly contain the word 'Twitter' from Elon, (2) run a broader latest-only search for terms he might use when discussing the platform without naming it, and (3) pull his timeline and profile metadata (rest_id) so we can follow tweet IDs into conversation endpoints in the next round. Additionally, retrieving who he follows will quickly reveal whether he still follows Twitter/X corporate accounts (useful signal) and suggest other accounts to monitor. This plan uses multiple endpoint types (search, timeline, profile, network) to satisfy the diversity requirement and to produce complementary outputs (IDs, context, network) for follow-up deep dives.

Graph Strategy: Previous rounds returned no direct hits for Elon Musk mentioning 'Twitter' and produced irrelevant search noise. The highest-priority gaps are (1) whether Elon Musk has recently posted anything that explicitly names Twitter, and (2) whether he has talked about platform-related topics without using the brand name (e.g., 'the platform', 'X', 'free speech', verification). To resolve these efficiently while obeying the diversity rule (avoid overusing search.php), the plan uses a single targeted search.php query that covers both explicit and likely alternative phrasings, plus complementary endpoints (timeline.php, screenname.php, trends.php) to verify account state, capture full recent chronology (including replies/threads), and check whether Twitter-related topics are trending in the U.S. This combination addresses the critical gaps, avoids repeated meaningless hits, and gives multiple angles for follow-up (tweet thread analysis, replies, or network checks).

Graph Strategy: We need to close the primary information gap: what exactly Elon Musk has been saying about Twitter (a.k.a. X) in the recent period. The investigation should (1) capture his direct statements that mention Twitter/X, (2) capture context from his full recent timeline to spot indirect statements or policy/operational announcements, (3) collect how others are framing or reacting to his Twitter/X comments, and (4) situate those statements against current public interest/trends. To do this efficiently and robustly, the next round must use multiple endpoints (not only search) to get primary-source tweets, profile metadata, broader public reaction, and trending context.

Graph Strategy: We need to quickly close the primary information gaps about what Elon Musk has been saying about Twitter/X in the most recent period. That requires: (1) collecting Elon Musk's raw posts (chronological) to see the full content and timing; (2) targeted searches that filter his posts for mentions of Twitter/X and related keywords so we don't miss brief references; (3) situational context (are Twitter/X topics trending) to understand reach and news hooks; and (4) follow-up conversation analysis on any high-impact tweet(s) to capture reactions and the narrative. To satisfy the investigation's diversity rule and the strategic imperative, this plan uses several complementary endpoints (timeline, targeted search, trends, profile verification, and conversation analysis) so the next round yields both breadth (all recent activity) and depth (threads and replies on key tweets). Some conversation endpoints are staged — they will be executed after step 1/2 identify specific tweet IDs.

Graph Strategy: We currently have no Twitter-derived data about Tesla stock (TSLA). The most coherent next step is to fill basic observational gaps: capture real-time public sentiment and news about TSLA, collect direct signals from the CEO and corporate account that often move the stock, check whether Tesla/TSLA is trending, and map a small network signal (who Elon follows) to identify likely influential sources/opinion leaders. This foundational collection will enable later deep dives (conversation threads, influencer mapping, quantitative sentiment tracking). The plan uses multiple endpoints to satisfy the diversity requirement and to provide complementary perspectives (search for broad public discourse, timelines for authoritative sources, trends to measure volume, and following for network context).

The next round should therefore (A) capture the latest tweet-level signals about BTC, (B) check trending topic context, and (C) pull recent posts and profile metadata for high-impact accounts so we can verify credibility and then deep-dive into any candidate tweets/threads. To satisfy the mandate to use diverse endpoints and to produce complementary results, I propose targeted search queries, trend checks, and profile/timeline pulls. These will produce candidate tweet IDs and accounts for follow-up conversation and network analysis in the next round.
